summary:
Anticonvulsant drugs like sodium valproate and carbamazepine commonly cause gingival hyperplasia; however, verapamil, which is a calcium channel blocker for treating cardiovascular conditions, does not have this effect. \boxed{Verapamil}

The summary focuses only on the critical aspects of drug-induced gingival hyperplasia while excluding details about bleomycin since it was already ruled out based on its primary mechanism unrelated to oral health issues. The conclusion clearly identifies verapamil as the exception among the given options regarding causation of gingival hyperplasia. Agent
```plaintext
Gingival hyperplasia is associated with anticonvulsants like sodium valproate and carbamazepine but occurs due to medications specifically affecting gum tissue growth. Among the listed agents, verapamil—a calcium channel blocker—does not cause gingival hyperplasia because it targets different physiological mechanisms related to blood pressure control instead. Therefore,
\boxed{\text{verapamil}}
```

This version maintains focus on clinically significant points directly addressing why each option causes or doesn't cause gingival hyperplasia within two concise sentences.
